lines.text: A single brushstroke on the wall. The calligrapher spent three years preparing to make it. The dungeon has been studying it since.
Characters that contain their own history — each stroke a compressed record of the hand that learned to make it, the teacher who corrected it, the generations before them.

The scroll case is lacquered black and very old. The seal bears a character that means, approximately, 'do not open this if you are not ready.' The dungeon considers the question of readiness ongoing.
Ink and brushstroke silence. The calligrapher's tower was built to hold the work of those who believed that the perfection of a single character was a lifetime's worth of achievement.
The tower has twelve levels. Each level represents one generation of the calligraphic tradition housed here. The dungeon is on the third level of the third generation of additions.
The scribe-ghost works in the corner. Not malicious — habitual. A thousand years of practice continues after the hand that held the brush has finished.
Silk and carved wood — materials chosen because they absorb without destroying. The characters here were meant to be permanent. The dungeon has ensured this.
The radical is the smallest meaningful unit. The dungeon organizes this level by radical — not by meaning, not by reading, but by the shape that underlies all the shapes.
A mistake in one of the scrolls — detected, acknowledged in the margins, left in place. The tradition required errors to be preserved alongside corrections. The dungeon agreed with this policy.
Every character carries its etymology visibly — the original meaning is present in the strokes, compressed, overlaid by centuries of refinement but never entirely erased.
